questions.text: Why is 'Loss Scaling' necessary when training neural networks using FP16 mixed precision, but not required when using BF16?
options: Because FP16 has a narrow 5-bit exponent range causing small gradients to underflow to zero, whereas BF16 has an 8-bit exponent width matching FP32's dynamic range.
Because FP16 requires more GPU memory than BF16, making gradient accumulation unstable.
Because BF16 uses strictly integer values, which cannot underflow.
Because FP16 activations are non-linear, while BF16 activations are linear.
questions.correct_answer: Because FP16 has a narrow 5-bit exponent range causing small gradients to underflow to zero, whereas BF16 has an 8-bit exponent width matching FP32's dynamic range.
questions.explanation: FP16 allocates only 5 bits for the exponent, restricting its representable minimum positive value to $\approx 6 \times 10^{-5}$. Small gradients underflow to zero unless scaled up. BF16 allocates 8 bits for the exponent (matching FP32), allowing it to represent values down to $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{-38}$, making loss scaling unnecessary.

questions.text: In mixed precision training, why are parameter weights maintained as an FP32 copy (master weights) during optimizer updates instead of updating in FP16 directly?
options: To prevent small parameter updates (product of learning rate and gradients) from underflowing to zero due to FP16's limited fraction bit precision.
To satisfy CPU scheduling architectures which do not support 16-bit calculations.
To enforce the Jenson-Shannon divergence loss constraint on weight values.
To allow the network to train without requiring any backward pass calculations.
questions.correct_answer: To prevent small parameter updates (product of learning rate and gradients) from underflowing to zero due to FP16's limited fraction bit precision.
questions.explanation: Because learning rates and gradients are small, their product (the weight delta $\eta \mathbf{g}$) is extremely small. If weights were stored in FP16, adding this tiny update to a relatively large weight value (e.g. 1.0) would fail due to limited fraction precision (underflow). Maintaining FP32 master weights preserves these updates.
